title: Systems and methods for identifying and remediating sound masking
abstract: Some embodiments of the invention are directed to enabling a user to easily identify the frequency range(s) at which sound masking occurs, and addressing the masking, if desired. In this respect, the extent to which a first stem is masked by one or more second stems in a frequency range may depend not only on the absolute value of the energy of the second stem(s) in the frequency range, but also on the relative energy of the first stem with respect to the second stem(s) in the frequency range. Accordingly, some embodiments are directed to modeling sound masking as a function of the energy of the stem being masked and of the relative energy of the masked stem with respect to the masking stem(s) in the frequency range, such as by modeling sound masking as loudness loss, a value indicative of the reduction in loudness of a stem of interest caused by the presence of one or more other stems in a frequency range.
claims: Claims 1. A method comprising acts of: (A) determining a loudness of a first audio recording at each of a plurality of frequencies during a time period; (B) determining a loudness of a second audio recording at each of the plurality of frequencies during the time period; (C) determining a partial loudness of the first audio recording at a first frequency of the plurality of frequencies during the time period, based at least in part on the loudness of the second audio recording, at the first frequency during the time period, determined in the act (B); (D) determining a loudness loss for the first audio recording at the first frequency during the time period based at least in part on the partial loudness determined in the act (C); and (E) enabling a user to apply one or more measures based upon the loudness loss determined in the act (D). 2. The method of claim 1, wherein the first audio recording and the second audio recording are each tracks of a multi-track recording. 3. The method of claim 1, wherein the first audio recording and the second audio recording are delimited to a time interval. 4. The method of claim 3, wherein the first audio recording and the second audio recording are synchronized during the time interval. 5. The method of claim 1, wherein the act (A) comprises transforming a digital representation of the first audio recording to the frequency domain, and generating a first excitation pattern using a result of the transforming. 6. The method of claim 5, wherein the transforming comprises transforming the digital representation using a Fourier transform having a variable frequency resolution across the plurality of frequencies. 7. The method of claim 1, wherein the act (A) comprises determining the loudness of the first audio recording at each of the plurality of frequencies using an audibility threshold and an extreme loudness threshold, the audibility threshold representing a minimum excitation level that a human ear perceives at each of the plurality of frequencies, the extreme loudness threshold representing a highest excitation level that a human ear tolerates at each of the plurality of frequencies. 8. The method of claim 1, wherein the loudness determined in the act (A) comprises at least one of an instantaneous loudness, a time-smoothed long-/short-term loudness, and a specific loudness. 9. The method of claim 1, wherein the act (C) comprises determining a partial loudness of the first audio recording at a first frequency of the plurality of frequencies during the time period based at least in part on (1) the loudness of the second audio recording during the given time period at the first frequency determined in the act (B) and (2) a masking threshold representing a minimum threshold of human audibility at each of the plurality of frequencies. 10. The method of claim 9, wherein the masking threshold is based at least in part upon a psychoacoustic model. 11. The method of claim 1, wherein the partial loudness determined in the act (C) comprises at least one of an instantaneous loudness, a time-smoothed long-/short-term loudness, and a specific loudness. 12. The method of claim 1, wherein the act (A) comprises determining a loudness at the first frequency measured in phons, the act (C) comprises determining a partial loudness at the first frequency measured in phons, and the act (D) comprises determining a loudness loss by subtracting the partial loudness determined in the act (C) from the loudness determined in the act (A). 13. The method of claim 1, wherein the act (E) comprises causing display of a graphical user interface depicting the loudness loss determined in the act (D), the graphical user interface enabling the user to provide input for at least partially remediating the loudness loss. 14. The method of claim 13, wherein the input relates to one or more measures comprising filtering, mixing, equalizing, attenuating, amplifying, panning, compressing and phase rotating at least one of the first digital representation and the second digital representation at the first frequency. 15. A method comprising acts of: (A) identifying a plurality of sound masking events in which sound in a first audio recording during a time period in a frequency range is masked by sound in a second audio recording during the time period in the frequency range, each sound masking event having an associated loudness loss defining a magnitude of the sound masking event, the loudness loss associated with each sound masking event being based at least in part on a difference between a loudness of the sound in the first audio recording occurring in isolation and a partial loudness of the sound in the first audio recording occurring concurrently with the sound in the second audio recording; (B) identifying a subset of the plurality of sound masking events as having a greater associated loudness loss than others of the plurality of sound masking events; and (C) causing a representation of the identified subset of the plurality of sound masking events to be displayed to a user on a graphical user interface. 16. The method of claim 15, wherein the act (B) comprises identifying sound masking events having an associated loudness loss in the 99 th percentile or greater. 17. The method of claim 15, wherein the act (B) comprises identifying sound masking events having an associated loudness loss in the 95 th percentile or greater. 18. The method of claim 15, wherein the act (B) comprises identifying sound masking events having an associated loudness loss in the 90 th percentile or greater.. 19. The method of claim 15, wherein the act (B) comprises identifying sound masking events having an associated loudness loss in the 80 th percentile or greater. 20. The method of claim 15, wherein the representation which is caused to be displayed in the act (C) is a bar graph in which each bar represents a number of sound masking events in the identified subset observed in a frequency range. 21. The method of claim 20, wherein each bar represents a number of sound masking events in the identified subset occurring within a predetermined amount of time prior to display. 22. The method of claim 20, wherein each bar represents a number of sound masking events in the identified subset occurring for at least a predetermined amount of time. 23. The method of claim 15, wherein the act (C) comprises causing display of a graphical user interface providing controls for adjusting equalization parameters for at least one of the first audio recording and the second audio recording. 24. The method of claim 23, wherein the controls enable a user to increase an amplitude of a filter applied to at least one of the first audio recording and the second audio recording in a frequency range, decrease an amplitude of a filter applied to at least one of the first audio recording and the second audio recording in a frequency range, and/or modify a center frequency at which a filter is applied to at least one of the first audio recording and the second audio recording. 25. The method of claim 15, wherein the act (C) comprises causing display of a graphical user interface providing controls for adjusting a threshold loudness loss defining how many sound masking events are included in the subset.
